## The Politics of Science

### Introduction

Politics may be understood in two ways: 1) Any decision process within a group of people (>=2). 2) The rupture of the common understanding (Jacques Rancière).

This lab unit contained both of the two understandings of politics. First we focused on the decision process of the two of us "A" and "D". A large amount of discussion time went into how to gain a common understanding and how to make decisions, thus politics by the understanding in 1).
Then, the lab unit started off, with the rupture of our common understanding of what a lab unit in the lab course was. The structure was stripped from around us and we experienced "politics" by form of the meaning 2).  

Goal of the two weeks period was shifted from usual goals of being performative in experiments to learning the process of scientific research, (which not only includes progress and decisive steps but also consensus over steps and mutual understanding of progress), hence whole activity was focused on learning research process and we used topic of ECDL as more of direction for our two weeks.

- The task that we were given was to assign ourself a task and observe ourselves and find issues that we had in the process to achieving our goal.
- The goal we assigned ourselves was to try to understand a cavity diode laser and simulate a simple model of it.
- Aim was to keep track of how we progress and how do we live through the differences and process of coming to consencus. This will lead us to find a way to level our understanding and bridge gaps we both have and how to check if we both mean the same thing when we are talking about any scientific term or process.

### Description 


#### Git repo structure

We, two people "A" and "D", were provided with an atomic physics experiment which allows students to perform the an experiment called "electromagnetically induced transparency". 
Within this framework the question that we must face is "What is it that we want to understand?"
Before we could tackle the task together, we needed a platform that allowed for independant working and thinking simultaneously capable to merge them into one project on common grounds.
The platform we chose was github.
As a new working environment it took some time and effort to get used to the environment:

- planning of repository structure
- basic workflow
- functionality of branches 
- merging and merge conflicts

We decided for the following structure of the repository:

```
data/
  orientation.json 
figures/
notes/
  pictures/
  sections/
  lab_notes.qmd
  awais.qmd
  dominic.qmd
report/
```

With the goal of to have a structure to allow for constructive collaboration of two people. 
The "data" directory is a location for all data, with "orientation.json" capable for keeping track of the potential filenames (as keys) and the metadata. The notes contain the lab notes.
With a folder for pictures and sub sections that can be included. The two files "awais.qmd" and "dominic.qmd" were for preventing merge conflicts and independant working files, with the idea of concluding and merging our common knowladge in "lab_notes.qmd". This proofed difficult as the following paragraphs will give insights to.


#### Finding a topic
What do we want to understand? – is the first step for any scientific work. We went about this, by looking at the components of our experiment: 

- laser physics: external-cavity diode-laser (ECDL)
- atomic physics: $^{87}\text{Rn}(I=3/2)$, $^{85}\text{Rn}(I=5/2)$
- optics: alignment and optical components
    - linear polarizer
    - quarter wave plate
    - half wave plate
    - lense
    - Fabry-Pérot interferometer
- spectroscopy techniques: Absorption, Flourescence, and Saturation
- interferometery: Fabry-Pérot interferometer

From these topics we identified our interests. During the discussion we realized that the underlying tool for our experiment is the external-cavity diode-laser (ECDL). A consensus was reached to study the external-cavity diode-laser in detail. It was the goal to get an in depth understanding of our device, and be able to manipulate the laser through controllable parameters. This, eventually led us to attempt a numerical simulation and modeling the ECDL. This decision process was finalized with a written document, that summarized our thoughts, discussions as well as holding onto concrete next steps.

In this discussion we came across a issues that highlight the recursive manner of scientific work. This is, that another fundamental tool of the experiment is required. The tool for obervations. These were the photo-diode and an interferometer, or Fabry-Pérot interferometer, which would be used to characterize the ECDL. Therefore, we faced the dilemma that required us to have a thorough understanding of these instruments, simultaneous to the understanding of the ECDL. This dilemma, though was not further discussed until the end of the two week lab time.

"We find it difficult to pin our interests onto one specific topic. We would like to set up an experiment capable of observing the atomic structure, i.e. to see the fine structure– or hyperfine structure splitting. To set up an experiment that is capable of measuring these incredibly detailed measurements of physical structures we need to focus on the tools that we use to analyse the atomic structure of Rubidium.

In our opinion, to make the extremely detailed measurement of a atomic structure, a detailed understanding of the external-cavity diode-laser (ECDL) and its emission spectrum is required. Detailed knowledge on the diode laser should provide us with the means to setup a ideal frequency to observe the fine–, hyperfine structure. But how do we characterize the light of the ECDL? 

This brings us to the issue on spectroscopy. The Fabry-Pérot interferometer only measures relative spectra – though very precisely. To correctly characterize the emitted light of the ECDL, it seems advantageous to gain a deep understanding and a characterization of the Fabry-Pérot interferometer, which allows for the full characterization of the diode laser. The characterization of the ECDL go hand-in-hand with the characterization of the Fabry-Pérot interferometer.

The last point to be considered is optics. Not all optical components need to be understood in great detail to characterize the ECDL, though some shall be essential, i.e. a lense as well as the diffraction grating on a mirror are an integral part of the ECDL.

The set goal should be to **fully characterize the ECDL**, which by necessity shall include the characterization of other parts to the setup of the experimental set up to EIT.
This means, we want to:

- compare emission spectrum of ECDL with theoretical prediction of semiconductor physics:
  - How does the population inversion take place?
  - What is our semi conductor made of (i.e. GaAs)?
  - Is a characterization of the semi-conductor possible ($I–U$)?
  - What instruments do we have to measure the spectrum?
  - What is the Free spectral range of the ECDL (Fabry-Pérot modes)?
  - Temperature dependance of emitted spectrum?
  - Can we determine the diffraction index $n$ of the depletion region (optical fiber)

As concrete next steps, we need to find out about material compositions and the dimensions of the diode to then understand the models that describe the emission spectrum to make numerical predictions."

When delving into the literature, we realized that some of the task we set ourselves, could not be achieved. It appeared, that the parameters and dimension of the diode, that were expected to be easily obtained were not given in the handbook. 

Retrospectively it would require to document this observation and to redefine our task. Though we felt the urge to continue the literature research. This issue will become apparent in the following section.


#### Literature
After deciding on studying the ECDL, we needed to find and read literature that would give us insight into how to gain understanding of the ECDL.
The literature research was inherently independent work. We took notes independantly, held a glossary independantly. Keeping in mind to discuss what we have read and to teach one another what we learned and bridge knowledge gaps between the two of us. In other words, to merge our knowlagde so that we could be on common ground.

We focused on literature around diode laser in general and particularly about DLpro the laser available to us and about the characterization of laser diodes.
Throughout this phase we communicated our progress loosely. We took notes and also followed the advice of keeping a glossary in our respective files - separately. Defining "important" concepts as well as variables.

This separation of work, of loosely communicating our struggles, resulted in deviating from our task rather than focusing on a clear goal.  
After reading different literature we still had issues communicating the information and bridging the gap to come to a consensus about our next step.


Finding "relevant" or "good" literature to read is something we struggled with. Searching for literature over internet is tricky, as much as we have a lot of information it also results in deviation from original task. 
The task we set ourselves does not perfectly match the literature we come across first. Then when starting to read, we found ourselves delving deeply into one specific topic. This conflicts with trying to obtain a broad understanding of our topic. As to reach our set goal.

After our literature review we tried to simulate a basic model (with help of AI), and we kept track of the parameter of our interest. After simulating a simple model, we realized that the geometric parameter of our interest i.e.: cavity-length was not available to us. The simulation also made us realize that the information and our understanding that we obtained only reveals a limited instight. The real device fluctuates, though model did not. Which lead to the question how to model the errors and fluctuations. This lead us to the conclusion to take experimental data from our laser DLpro.

#### Second Week Direction

Try to have numerical model of these parameter and play around with the inputs and observe dynamics of output and understand the behaviour, and in mean while take data of inputs from real experimental setup and observe if our understanding of dynamics make sense or not, and what
pieces of puzzle are we missing.


##### Experimental Attempt

We started our experiment by taking data of intensity power (with use of photodiode), by varying the current, the other measureable parameter was peizo current (which is related to length of cavity). During our experient and tracking data we realized that the peizo voltage was not stable, it was fluctuating and drifting, leading to change in threshold current, and change in central mode. which made us realize that our output was influenced by two parameters. This lead us to have discussion and question our understanding of our ECDL setup. few of the points we wanted to revist were, how external cavity is affected if we turn peizo off, would it favour us?, would it be better to find optimal hopfree mode?, and what should be our next step, and what do we want to achieve?


### Reflection and Issues

Our two-week journey was never short of difficulties. This made us realize how difficult the scientific process itself could be. Among all other major and minor issues, a prominent one was the drive and motivation to stay optimistic every day.

Our backgrounds as individuals clearly affect our understanding. A different upbringing should not be taken lightly: “D” grew up in Germany with a bilingual English/German upbringing, while “A” grew up in Pakistan in a multilingual surrounding. 
In a physics project our interests as physicists gave rise to the starting points.  These needed to be agreed upon and merged in order to work on the same problem. Luckily, the two of us have a similar interest in physics. We studied similar courses with interests in abstract mathematical concepts. Hence our choice for a deeper theoretical understanding of the ECDL.

One of the core issues we can pinpoint was finding structure in our progress during times of disagreement. When we did not set anything as definite truth, all statements seemed to become equal truths. This made it difficult to decide how to move forward.

This difficulty was connected to the way the two-week activity was redefined for us. Usually, such an activity might be understood as working toward a clear goal, result, or presentation. However, UW told us there were no expectations, which made it both tricky and difficult to understand what our direction should be. The impression we had was that the activity was about learning the learning-process, which made us look inward. An inward looking, introspective task. One is usually introspective toward past actions, not present ones. Trying to be introspective while still being inside the process made us more aware of ourselves. Though also unsure about what to do next, and how to assess ourselves while doing it.

Being used to expectations and the developed habit to fullfill the expectations of our superior under timeconstraints could bot be overlooked. Whilst trying adopt to the new structure, the time constraint remained. Which toward the deadline resulted in the difficulty of maintaining motivation and optimism. This became especially clear, when progress, in the sense of advancing, seeing results, or having something to present to a third person, was not reached. 
Furthermore in a time-constrained working environment, one cannot explore and deviate much. The feeling of having little time left for any further work led us to stutter and come to a halt. At that point, the lack of confidence in our knowledge was prominent, and simple philosophical questions made us question the most basic thing: What are we doing here? And why are we doing what we were doing? 

Throughout the entire process, a recurring conflict that we observed and tried to understand was our expectations of ourselves as individuals. However, what exactly these differences were, and where they came from, was not easy to identify. So far, we can formulate them through two statements. On the one hand: “I feel like I am free, so I want to make the most out of this opportunity.” On the other hand: “I felt like, as there are no expectations, I would just want to enjoy the process of learning without really worrying about doing a lot.” This made us question, how we could find a middle ground in terms of our expectations?

Another important issue was about communication: How to express thoughts in the best possible way? How to make sure that information was transferred clearly and without too much deviation? As well as, how to know whether we had reached the same understanding? This also raised the question of how we come to agreement when our interpretations of certain concepts are not exactly the same.

This became especially visible when trying to define concepts like “semiconductor.” Our language background, mentioned above, defined our boundary conditions. To agree upon the same problem, the problem needs to be defined without missunderstanding.

A definition is given in relation to common words and technical words, which, when defined, are related to other common words and technical words, and so on and so forth. In the end, one creates a web of relations that defines technical terms through this relation in terms of common words. Common words are those that seem natural and need no further explanation, for example:

semiconductor
: A material that can be tuned, i.e. voltage V, to switch between two macroscopic behaviors: insulator and conductor.

Common words here could arguably be “material,” “switch,” or “behaviors.” Of course, this can maybe be avoided by defining these terms as well, though there are two issues. One is the practicality under time constraints whilst wanting to learn physics. Secondly, there is the question about the actual possibility of clearly defining without the use of technical terms that need no definition, which we, the authors, do not believe is possible. Further investigation of this would be interesting.

Our differences were not only scientific but also aesthetic in nature. At times, our disagreements were not about right or wrong, but about different perspectives. In terms of aesthetics, we felt that we both had different preferences, which expressed themselves in our expectations of progress and were also visible in the glossary section while coming to consensus about definitions. In that sense, we did not feel that either of us was right or wrong, but these differences still influenced the goal we wanted to achieve and how we could form consensus over it? More importantly, this made us question, how these expectations reflected in our struggle to work scientifically together? And what we could do to overcome this?

Progress got sidetracked frequently. By the idea of creating a numerical simulation for future students. As well as a deep dive into one specific literature, rather than collecting various literature. This bahaviour can be explained by us ignoring the question if this aligns with the way we wanted to proceed originally. Which resulted in an observable change within our notes, as details on “how to use the DLpro” or “tuning of the diode laser” were gathered in the hope of being helpful to others in the future. Also, “important” notes such as graphs by the manufacturer, capturing the input and output parameters, were collected. 


### Conclusion

- this experimental class was about how to work scientifically.
- this was a big break and contrast to the usual school like working environment: fullfilling the expectations of the seniors or superiors, in order to pass or obtain good grades
- eventhough we appreciated the change we struggeled to really adapt to this change, as the old habits seem to run deep

- we considered our working environment which sets preliminaries of our team work as important though also faulty. Our lack of experience with Github coast a few days. Our struggle can be seen particuly in the history of the branches
- the structure of the repository would considere this: i.e. by having a better structure in the notes, that does not have a linear one way structure but maybe inlcudes pages for different topics. Pages that have unstructured thought though take note of date and time of edits. As well as pages that have structured thought, and are organized by topics. Then pages that keep track of organized notes. Clearly presenting changes to previous entries, capturing the main line of argumentation, describing where we find ourselves and where we wish to go.

- we described how we came to a conclusion for the topic, this progress was summarized in written form. With observing that the continouse progress deviated to the set goal by ourselves when continuing in the literature review. Not only did we deviate from our own goals we defined, but also did our common knowlagde. We worked independantly not taking the time to see: Are we in line with our previous tasks? No! So what changed? Then rephrase our question, in order to orientate ourselves – in order not to "vor lauter Bäume den Wald nicht sehen". 

- For the second week we did define how to proceed.
- though we did not bridge the gap that developed in our knowladge
- from here on the our work became chaotic our ideas and motives changed 
- we started a measurement which resulted in more uncertainty
- our goals we set ourselves were clearly not appropriate to the time constraint
- our notes did not catch up with or discussions
- we came to realize that the knowladge gap posed an issue for further advancements

- then we gave insight into our thoughts and reflection on the two week lab unit.

- there we can takeaway that it could be advantageous to put in more efforts for taking notes, structuring our thoughts and how what goals we expect to do next. This may also help with finding goals that meet the time constraints. It should allow for time allocated required for creatively following ones interests, so that one may get sidetracked but to become aware of this.

- The scientific progress is slow and draining, and expectation of quick results wouldnt help.

- Better Notes: One way to make proper progress is to have two set of notes, one with unordered thoughts and explaination, and then develope on it into seperate notes which are more organized, and structured.(it is very retrospective way to take notes) 

- We realized in the process having realistic goals can always be helpful, and having specific goal which are simple can always help.(having grand goal always helps you to keep on exploring more, but we realized people need small achievements to keep them motivated and keep on going.)

- While working independently it is important to have more of discussion sessions where we can explain eachother about what we learned, this would have helped in two manners; one is building the bridge of understandind, and second is it would have helped in being confident over our understand of what we read.

- The issues of ego if are addressed while working can help to make working enviornment healthy. But it also depends on the people one is working with.

One way to make proper progress is to have two set of notes, one with unordered thoughts and explaination, and then develope on it into seperate notes which are more organized, and structured.(it is very retrospective way to take notes)

"It is good to be in this lost state of mind because this is the only state that truly exists, though we usually trick ourselves into thinking otherwise" – is what we said on the last day in the lab course. This addresses the hierarcal structure which imposes the objective rights or wrongs, urges us to act as if we were fully understanding and to display confidence. This also captures our aesthetic toward life, in particular towards scientific work itself: That we are all lost wonderers finding our way.
Though the way university and its courses are structured such that this may not be embraced. 
As consequence the variety of ideas decreases; we are required to work to meet expectations, which results in narrow mindedness, strengthening its own hierarchies and enforces conformist attitude in scientific community.
We wish to embrace creativity! We wish to break hierarchies! We wish to stop lying to ourselves and to one another! We are lost! We wish to be open to all possibilities!

#### Consensus in Scientific work
Consensus on Wikipedia is defined as a general agreement, accord, or harmony in opinion or attitude among a group.

Consensus itself is one of the intrinsic features in scientific work; during our two weeks of EIT experiment, we spent time pondering that question of consensus and also about how to agree when we are not dictated to by right or wrong by a supervisor . As for the two-week period we were given total freedom, which means there were no expectations to hold up either. This reminds me of a statement by Kafka: 'I am free, and that is why I am lost.' Which was how we felt for most of two weeks.

We both agreed that in order to make scientific progress, one needs a system of active input and a feedback loop. (We defined progress as active agreement between person "A" and "D", not progress in a performative sense.)

**(We need to define our system of active input and a feedback loop)**: This system is kind of underlying; without actively working on it, **input** is we first try to define (one needs to explain what it means to say we define something) one thing as we understand it, and the person starts to ask questions around their doubts about how we have defined the thing. Which I consider **feedback**.

This is more of a reflection on our two weeks and suggestions on how this could have been better and could be followed for our future endeavours.


- The most valuable skill one can ever learn is ability to figure it out

- things that we would do differently if we could are; having more short term achievable goals.


#-----------------

This experimental class was about learning how to work scientifically. It was a strong contrast to the usual school-like working environment, where students often fulfil the expectations of superiors in order to pass or obtain good grades. Even though this change was appreciated, it was difficult to fully adapt to it, as old habits seemed to run deep.

One of the central questions that emerged during the two weeks was the question of consensus in scientific work. Consensus itself is one of the intrinsic features of scientific work, especially when there is no supervisor dictating what is right or wrong. During the lab, there was a large amount of freedom, which also meant that there were no clear expectations to hold up. This reminded of Kafka’s statement: “I am free, and that is why I am lost.” In retrospect, this describes the feeling for most of the two weeks.

The group realized that scientific progress requires a system of active input and feedback. Progress not in a performative sense, but as active agreement between the people. Active input meant that one person tried to define something as they understood it, while feedback meant that the other person asked questions, expressed doubts, and challenged how that thing had been defined. In this sense, consensus was not simply agreement, but a process of explanation, questioning, correction, and reformulation.

The working environment was therefore seen as important, but also faulty. It set the preliminaries for teamwork, but did not automatically create the conditions for clear consensus. The lack of experience with GitHub cost the group a few days, and this struggle can be seen particularly in the history of the branches. A clearer repository and note-taking structure could have supported the work better. Instead of a linear, one-way structure, the notes could have included one space for unordered thoughts and explanations, and another for more organized notes that captured the main line of argumentation, changes in thinking, and the group’s current direction.

This lack of structure was also visible in the development of the topic itself. While continuing the literature review, the group’s progress deviated from the goal that had been set. Both members worked independently without taking enough time to ask whether they were still in line with previous tasks. For the second week, the group did define how to proceed, but the knowledge gap that had developed was not properly bridged. After that, the effort got increasingly disorganised, motivations and concepts shifted, and rather than eliminating the uncertainty, the measurement created more questions. In retrospect, the group should have paused more often, rephrased the research question, and asked what had changed in order not to “den Wald vor lauter Bäumen nicht sehen.”

From this experience, the group realized that realistic goals and regular exchange are important for building consensus. Having a grand goal can help to keep exploring, but people also need small achievements to stay motivated and keep going. More short-term, achievable goals would therefore have helped the group to remain oriented. It would also have been important to have more discussion sessions in which both members explained to each other what they had learned. This would have helped by building a bridge of understanding and by making both members more confident in what they had read.

Scientific progress is slow and draining, and the expectation of quick results does not help. The two-week experience showed that uncertainty is not necessarily a failure, but it needs structure, communication, and honest reflection in order to become productive. Issues of ego, if addressed while working, can also help to make the working environment healthier, though this depends on the people one is working with.

On the last day of the lab course, the group said: “It is good to be in this lost state of mind because this is the only state that truly exists, though we usually trick ourselves into thinking otherwise.” This sentence captures an important part of the reflection. It addresses the hierarchical structure which imposes objective rights or wrongs, urges students to act as if they fully understand, and makes them display confidence. However, real scientific work often begins from being lost. The problem is not uncertainty itself, but the pressure to hide it.

The way university and its courses are structured may not always allow this state to be embraced. As a consequence, the variety of ideas may decrease; students are required to work in order to meet expectations, which can result in narrow-mindedness, strengthen hierarchies, and enforce a conformist attitude in the scientific community. Against this, the group wished to embrace creativity, to stop lying to themselves and to one another, and to remain open to all possibilities.

The most valuable skill one can learn is the ability to figure it out. In future work, the group would therefore aim for short-term achievable goals, better structured notes, and regular discussion sessions in order to build consensus while working through uncertainty.
